summary: bin/rubocop is clean (145 files, no offenses). Ran the full Minitest suite in the test environment, including system tests (RAILS_ENV=test bin/rails test:all, PARALLEL_WORKERS=1 to avoid a known fork-safety segfault in this Ruby/libpq/pg combination when running parallel workers): 185 runs, 662 assertions, 1 failure, 0 errors, 0 skips. The one failure is test/system/theme_toggle_test.rb:7 (`assert_not dark_mode?` right after sign-in). Root cause: this machine's OS is set to Dark mode (`defaults read -g AppleInterfaceStyle` -> Dark), headless Chrome inherits that as `prefers-color-scheme: dark`, and the app correctly defaults to the system preference per the theme_controller.js / _theme_init_script.html.erb logic -- so the app behaves as specified, but the system test hardcodes an assumption that the ambient/default theme is light without pinning the emulated color scheme, making the test's outcome depend on the host machine's OS-level appearance setting rather than being deterministic. This is a real, currently-reproducing failure (not flaky-in-theory) and will fail identically on any CI runner or dev machine set to dark mode. The prior test-critic iteration (3) passed because it ran only `bin/rails test`, which excludes system tests by default, so this gap was never previously exercised. No development or production database was touched -- only bin/rubocop and bin/rails test:all (RAILS_ENV=test) were run; dependencies were installed locally via `bundle config set --local path vendor/bundle` (no system/global gem writes).

findings.issue: The test asserts `assert_not dark_mode?` immediately after sign-in, assuming the browser's default color-scheme preference is light, but never emulates/pins `prefers-color-scheme` for the Capybara/Selenium session. On a host (or CI runner) whose OS appearance is set to Dark, headless Chrome reports `prefers-color-scheme: dark`, the app correctly applies dark mode per its 'default to system settings' requirement, and this assertion fails (confirmed reproducing on this machine: `defaults read -g AppleInterfaceStyle` = Dark). Fix by explicitly forcing a known color scheme for the test session (e.g. a Chrome option/CDP call to set `prefers-color-scheme: light` in test/application_system_test_case.rb) so the test is deterministic regardless of the host's OS theme.
